title: Achyronia jacobaea
intro: Achyronia jacobaea, also known as Common Jacobaea or Jacobaea maritima, is a plant species in the sunflower family that is native to the coastal regions of Europe. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, as well as potential antimicrobial and antiviral activities. The bioactive constituents of Achyronia jacobaea include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various health issues, including respiratory problems and gastrointestinal disorders.
taxonomy: Achyronia jacobaea is a synonym for the plant species Jacobaea aquatica, but it is often confused with Jacobaea vulgaris, which is also known as the common ragwort. However, the correct classification of Achyronia jacobaea is not widely recognized or agreed upon in botanical sources. The species is thought to be a member of the dandelion family (Asteraceae), but its exact classification is uncertain. It is possible that Achyronia jacobaea is a synonym for another plant species within the Asteraceae family. Further research is needed to clarify the classification of Achyronia jacobaea.
uses: Achyronia jacobaea, commonly known as Jacobaea or Ragwort, has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, making it useful for treating wounds and skin conditions. The plant's extracts are also thought to have a potential role in treating fever and respiratory issues. However, more research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal effects and potential side effects. In some cultures, the plant is also used as a treatment for rheumatism and other joint-related conditions.

constituents: Achyronia jacobaea, also known as the ragwort plant, contains a variety of active constituents that contribute to its medicinal properties. These active constituents include sesquiterpene lactones, such as jacobin, desacetylbilacin, and 11β-hydroxy Jacobin.
